title: Other Social Science
intro: Questions in Other Social Science draw heavily on international relations theory, with the realist-liberal-constructivist debate forming the unit's densest cluster. Core concepts like anarchy, power, hegemony, and security recur alongside the theorists who formalized them: Kenneth Waltz's neorealism, Robert Keohane's neoliberal institutionalism, John Mearsheimer's offensive realism, and Alexander Wendt's constructivism. Nuclear deterrence and game theory, associated especially with Thomas Schelling, form a related strategic-studies thread.

A second major cluster covers domestic political institutions and processes — democracy, elections, voting, political parties, federalism, and gerrymandering — often studied comparatively across named states such as Canada, China, Japan, and Israel, which recur as case studies in both comparative politics and international relations. Francis Fukuyama's thesis on the endpoint of liberal democracy bridges this cluster with IR theory.

Law questions form a distinct thread built around civil and criminal doctrine: torts, contracts, negligence, mens rea, common law, criminal law, copyright, and the role of juries, generally tested through definitional and case-based clues rather than named jurists. Media and communications studies contribute a third cluster anchored by Marshall McLuhan and Walter Lippmann, with propaganda, television, and public opinion as recurring subjects.

Sociology and social theory appear through canonical texts and concepts — Karl Marx's foundational class analysis, Judith Butler's Gender Trouble, Benedict Anderson's Imagined Communities, and debates over feminism and heterosexuality as social institutions — while education (Maria Montessori) and business/management, including McDonald's as a globalization case study, round out the unit's remaining answerlines.

sections.name: International Relations Theory
sections.blurb: Core concepts and schools of IR — realism, neorealism, liberal institutionalism, and constructivism — and the theorists who built them.

entries.note: Absence of a central global authority above sovereign states; foundational assumption shared by realist and liberal IR theory.
Central IR concept denoting capacity to influence others' behavior; analyzed as hard, soft, or structural power.
School of IR emphasizing state self-interest, anarchy, and power politics; associated with Morgenthau, Waltz, and Mearsheimer.
American political scientist and leading offensive realist; author of The Tragedy of Great Power Politics.
Central to deterrence theory and mutually assured destruction; studied by Thomas Schelling and Kenneth Waltz on stability.
Central IR subject studied through causal theories such as the democratic peace and power transition theory.
American political scientist, founder of neorealism; author of Theory of International Politics and Man, the State, and War.
Structural realist school founded by Kenneth Waltz, emphasizing the anarchic international system over unit-level factors.
Sovereign political entity and primary unit of IR analysis; per Weber, holds a monopoly on legitimate force within its territory.
IR school holding that state identities and interests are socially constructed; associated with Alexander Wendt.
IR school emphasizing cooperation, institutions, and interdependence, contrasted with realism; associated with Robert Keohane.
Central IR concept spanning national and international security studies, including securitization theory.
Dominance of one state or actor over a system; central to hegemonic stability theory and Gramsci's cultural hegemony.
Academic discipline studying interactions between states, encompassing realism, liberalism, and constructivism.
American political scientist, co-founder of neoliberal institutionalism; co-authored Power and Interdependence with Joseph Nye.
IR school holding that state identities and interests are shaped by shared ideas and norms rather than fixed material power.
Concept coined by Joseph Nye describing influence through attraction and persuasion rather than coercion.
Sovereign entities that are the primary unit of analysis in international relations theory.
American economist and game theorist, Nobel laureate for work on strategic behavior and nuclear deterrence; wrote The Strategy of Conflict.

sections.name: Comparative Politics & Democracy
sections.blurb: Institutions and processes of democratic government: elections, parties, federalism, and the ideologies that animate them.

entries.note: Organizations that aggregate interests and contest elections; party systems classified as two-party, dominant-party, or multiparty.
Government based on popular sovereignty and free elections; contrasted with autocracy and measured by indices like Freedom House.
Mechanism of political participation studied through electoral systems, turnout patterns, and rational choice models.
Formal process of selecting representatives, studied via electoral system design, turnout, and campaign behavior.
Studied comparatively, notably in Democratic Peace Theory's claim that they rarely fight one another.
Manipulation of electoral district boundaries to favor a party, via techniques like packing and cracking.
Political ideology emphasizing individual rights, limited government, and free markets; contrasted with conservatism and realism.
System dividing power between central and regional governments, exemplified by the United States and Canada.
American political scientist known for "The End of History and the Last Man," arguing liberal democracy is history's ideological endpoint.
Studied through theories of voting behavior, party identification, and rational choice models.

sections.name: Law & Legal Systems
sections.blurb: Civil and criminal law concepts spanning torts, contracts, criminal liability, and the common-law tradition.

entries.note: Branch of civil law addressing civil wrongs such as negligence, covering duty of care and liability for damages.
Area of civil law governing legally binding agreements, requiring offer, acceptance, and consideration.
Latin for "guilty mind"; the mental-state element of criminal liability, contrasted with actus reus.
Legal system based on judicial precedent, originating in England, contrasted with civil law systems.
Legal protection granting authors exclusive rights over creative works, central to intellectual property law and policy.
Tort standard based on failure to exercise reasonable care, requiring duty, breach, causation, and damages.
Body of law defining crimes and punishments, distinguished from civil law by concepts like mens rea and actus reus.
Bodies of citizens who decide facts at trial, central to common-law adversarial legal systems.
Subject of legal debates over obscenity and free speech, notably in Catharine MacKinnon's feminist legal theory.
Subject of victimology, studying the psychological, social, and legal treatment of crime victims.

sections.name: Media & Communications Studies
sections.blurb: Theorists and concepts explaining how mass media, propaganda, and public opinion shape society and politics.

entries.note: Canadian media theorist who coined "the medium is the message" and "global village"; wrote Understanding Media and The Gutenberg Galaxy.
Broad object of study covering mass communication's effects on public opinion, framing, and agenda-setting.
Communication used to influence public opinion, studied via Edward Bernays and Walter Lippmann on opinion manipulation.
Broadcast medium studied for its effects on political communication, public opinion, and cultivation theory.
Studied for effects on behavior and political socialization, and as a medium of mass communication.
American journalist and commentator, author of Public Opinion (1922), which introduced "stereotype" in its social science sense.

sections.name: Sociology & Social Theory
sections.blurb: Social theory and critical thought on class, gender, and nationhood, from Marx to Butler to Anderson.

entries.note: German philosopher and economist (b. 1818), founder of Marxism and historical materialism; co-wrote The Communist Manifesto and Das Kapital.
Social and political movement/theory analyzing gender inequality, spanning waves from suffrage to Judith Butler's queer theory.
1990 book by Judith Butler introducing the concept of gender performativity, foundational to queer theory.
Studied as a social institution and construct, notably via Adrienne Rich's concept of "compulsory heterosexuality."
1983 book by Benedict Anderson arguing nations are socially constructed communities imagined through print capitalism.
Studied as a legal and social institution, including debates over its legal definition and same-sex marriage.

sections.name: Education & Pedagogy
sections.blurb: Educational theory and pedagogical methods, centered on child-centered and progressive approaches to schooling.

entries.note: Italian physician and educator who developed the Montessori Method, a child-centered pedagogy of self-directed, hands-on learning.
Field covering pedagogy, curriculum, and schooling systems, studied by theorists such as Montessori and Dewey.

sections.name: Business, Management & Globalization
sections.blurb: Organizational management theory and case studies in globalization and cultural standardization.

entries.note: Field concerned with organizing and directing organizations, including theories of leadership and organizational behavior.
Case study in globalization and standardization, notably George Ritzer's "McDonaldization" thesis.

sections.name: Countries & Case Studies
sections.blurb: Nations and cities recurring as comparative case studies across political science and sociology.

entries.note: North American federal parliamentary democracy studied for its multiparty system, bilingualism, and federal structure.
Studied comparatively for authoritarian one-party governance and rapid state-led economic development.
Studied for its parliamentary system, long LDP dominant-party rule, and postwar constitution.
Studied for parliamentary coalition politics and its central role in Middle Eastern international relations.
Studied as a global city and case study in urban politics and governance.
